task: I’m looking over a run where a few readings and labels don’t quite line up with the earlier records, and I want to make sure I’m not missing a developing issue. What’s a practical way to keep track of it over time and decide when it needs a closer look?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — I’d handle it as a **trend check, not a one-off judgment**.

### Practical way to track it
Use a simple log for each run or check:
- **date / time**
- **part or lot ID**
- **reading / label / record value**
- **who checked it**
- **same gauge / same method?**
- **pass / borderline / mismatch**
- **notes on anything unusual**  
  - operator
  - setup
  - temperature
  - vibration
  - rework
  - label changes

### What to watch for
You’re looking for:
- **repeatable drift** in the same direction
- **the same mismatch showing up again**
- **errors tied to one machine, one operator, or one lot**
- **borderline results becoming more frequent**
- **changes after heat, motion, or setup changes**

### When it needs a closer look
Escalate if:
- the same issue shows up **more than once**
- the mismatch is **getting larger**
- it follows the **equipment** instead of the part
- you can’t explain it with a known process change
- the reading is close enough to spec that a small shift could push it out

### Good rule
Don’t wait for a hard failure if you’re seeing a pattern.  
If the records are starting to **drift, repeat, or cluster around the same condition**, that’s the point to stop and investigate.

### Simple decision rule
- **One isolated mismatch** → log it, recheck, keep watching
- **Two or more similar mismatches** → start a closer review
- **Same issue across runs/lots** → treat it as a developing problem and investigate root cause

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page tracking sheet** with columns and a simple escalation trigger.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much the right direction. I’d want it a little tighter, though — a simple log is fine, but I’d also want a hard trigger for when it stops being “keep watching” and becomes “stop and verify.”

Something like:
- record the reading, label, lot, time, machine, and who checked it
- mark anything borderline or reworked
- compare each new run against the last few, not just the last one
- flag the same miss if it shows up twice in a short span

What I’d still want from you is a cleaner rule for escalation. For example: at what point do you call it a trend versus just noise? Two hits in a row? Same machine three times? That part matters.

turns.agent_response:
A practical rule is:

### Keep watching
- **1 isolated mismatch** that can be explained or doesn’t repeat

### Escalate to verify
- **2 similar misses in a short span** on the **same machine, same lot type, or same check point**
- **1 borderline result plus 1 repeat miss** on the same condition
- **any miss that keeps following the same setup, operator, or equipment**

### Call it a trend
Treat it as a trend if you see:
- the **same issue twice**
- in a **short window**
- under the **same or very similar conditions**
- especially if the gap is **getting larger** or the borderline cases are stacking up

### Simple shop-floor trigger
If it happens **once**, log it.  
If it happens **twice in the same pattern**, **stop and verify**.  
If it happens **three times or across multiple runs/lots**, treat it as a developing issue and investigate root cause.

That keeps you from chasing noise, but it also stops you from waiting until it turns into a real problem.

If you want, I can turn that into a **tight yes/no decision rule** you could drop straight into a work instruction.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the rule I’d use. 

Once is noise unless it’s ugly. Twice in the same pattern — same machine, same check point, same lot type — that’s when I’d stop and verify. Three times, or it starts showing up across runs, then it’s not noise anymore. That’s a trend.

I’d keep it simple on the floor:
- 1 miss: log it, watch it
- 2 similar misses in the same pattern: verify now
- 3 or more, or spread across runs/lots: treat it as a real process issue

That’s clean enough to use without arguing about every little blip.
